title: Kubernetes Pod CrashLoopBackOff
resolution_steps: Run: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace> to identify the failure reason
Check logs: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> --previous
Verify resource limits are not too restrictive: kubectl get pod <pod-name> -o yaml | grep -A5 resources
Check if required secrets/configmaps exist: kubectl get secrets,configmaps -n <namespace>
If liveness probe is failing, increase initialDelaySeconds or adjust thresholds
Redeploy with corrected configuration: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<deployment-name>

title: Jenkins Pipeline Build Failure
resolution_steps: Review the Jenkins build log for the specific stage that failed
Check if Nexus/Artifactory is reachable from the build agent
Verify credentials are present: Jenkins > Manage Jenkins > Credentials
Review SonarQube quality gate results if the quality stage failed
Check build agent resource utilization (CPU/memory)
Re-run the pipeline after addressing the root cause
If flaky test, investigate test isolation and add retry logic

title: AWS EC2 Instance High CPU
resolution_steps: Check CloudWatch metrics for CPU utilization trend over last 1-6 hours
SSH into the instance and run: top or htop to identify the consuming process
Review application logs for error spikes or unusual activity
If traffic spike: enable Auto Scaling or scale up the instance type
If runaway process: identify PID and investigate before killing
Consider moving to a compute-optimized instance type (C-series) if persistent
Set CloudWatch alarm for CPU > 80% for proactive alerting

title: Terraform Apply Failure
root_causes: State file lock from previous failed run
Resource limit reached in AWS account
resolution_steps: Run: terraform plan to review what changes will be made before applying
If state lock: terraform force-unlock <lock-id> after confirming no active runs
Check IAM role permissions for the Terraform execution role
Review AWS service quotas if resource limit error
Run: terraform validate to check for configuration syntax errors
Check provider version constraints in versions.tf
Review terraform.tfstate for any corrupted resources and use terraform import if needed

title: Prometheus Alert — Service Down
resolution_steps: Check Grafana dashboard for the affected service to identify when it went down
Verify pod/container is running: kubectl get pods -n <namespace>
Check application logs for OOM or crash signals
Verify network policies allow traffic on the required ports
Check if dependent services (database, cache, external API) are healthy
Review recent deployments that may have introduced the regression
Restart the service if no root cause found and document for RCA

title: Helm Deployment Rollout Stuck
root_causes: Readiness probe failing on new pods
resolution_steps: Check rollout status: kubectl rollout status deployment/<name> -n <namespace>
Describe pending pods: kubectl describe pod <pod-name> for scheduling errors
Check node resource availability: kubectl top nodes
Review PodDisruptionBudget: kubectl get pdb -n <namespace>
If image pull issue: verify image tag exists in registry and pull secret is valid
Rollback if needed: helm rollback <release-name> <revision>
Check readiness probe configuration and increase failureThreshold if needed
